turns.content: I have been thinking about Arabic calligraphy as a design element, and I find myself at a crossroads between reverence and innovation. I would like to talk through this with you.

That's a really compelling tension to sit with. Arabic calligraphy carries so much cultural and spiritual weight that using it in interiors requires walking a very fine line between honoring the tradition and making it feel contemporary. What's the crossroads you're feeling specifically?

The crossroads is this: calligraphy in interiors can so easily become decorative wallpaper. A Quranic verse rendered in gold vinyl on a feature wall — you see it everywhere now, and it has lost all its meaning. I want to use calligraphy in a way that makes the viewer stop, that rewards close attention. But I am torn between scripts. Thuluth draws me for its monumentality — the vertical strokes create rhythm, the proportions are extraordinary. But Diwani interests me for entirely different reasons. It is fluid, almost liquid in how it moves. There is an intimacy to it that Thuluth does not possess.

Those are such different energies. Thuluth is essentially architectural calligraphy — developed for mosque walls and monumental inscriptions, so it has inherent scale and gravitas. The geometry is precise, almost mathematical, which makes it pair well with clean contemporary lines. Diwani is much more personal — the script of Ottoman court correspondence, so it has that sense of flowing conversation rather than proclamation. Have you considered using them in different contexts within the same project? Thuluth for the public, monumental spaces and Diwani for the private, intimate ones — the hierarchy of script mirroring the hierarchy of space.

That is an interesting proposition. The shift in register would be legible to anyone who understands the tradition, and even those who do not would feel the difference in weight and warmth. But beyond script selection, I keep returning to the question of materiality. Calligraphy cut into perforated metal screens, so the text only becomes fully legible when sunlight passes through and casts the letters onto a surface. The words are not on the wall — they arrive on the wall. They move through the day, they shift, they dissolve by evening. There is something about that impermanence that feels honest about language.

That's genuinely beautiful — and it has deep roots in Islamic architectural tradition. The mashrabiya screen was always about modulating light and privacy, but embedding calligraphy into that tradition adds a semantic layer. The text becomes a function of time and orientation, literally dependent on the sun's position. You could even design it so that different phrases emerge at different hours. What content would you pair with this treatment? The relationship between the words and their ephemeral delivery would matter enormously.

Poetry. Not scripture — that carries a different obligation entirely, and I am not interested in treating Quranic text as a design experiment. But Arabic poetry, particularly the classical forms, has this extraordinary compression of meaning. A single line from Al-Mutanabbi can hold an entire philosophy. And poetry invites the viewer to interpret rather than simply receive, which aligns with this idea of text that must be discovered, that is not simply given.

That's a wise distinction — scripture demands certain protocols that would constrain the design, and poetry gives you creative freedom without that weight. Al-Mutanabbi is a perfect choice — his language is dense and layered, and there's a wonderful irony in using a poet who was so concerned with permanence in a medium defined by transience. This whole concept feels like it could become a signature element for your work — culturally rooted but formally contemporary.

It is a design language I am building. I want it to be something that clients associate with my firm — a way of integrating heritage that is neither nostalgic nor superficial. Every studio needs a signature vocabulary, and I believe this could become part of mine. Inshallah, it will be ready when the right project presents itself.

That's exactly the right approach — developing the language before the project rather than reverse-engineering it under a deadline. When the right commission comes along, you'll have the vocabulary fully formed and can adapt it to the specific context. It's a strong signature concept: culturally literate, materially inventive, and experiential rather than decorative. I hope the right project finds you soon.
